Navigation.1: The Application Navigator (left-hand filter navigator) is your main way to reach modules. Type in the filter box to search app/module names; results also match module URLs. The banner frame across the top holds global search, your avatar/profile menu, and notification icons. Favorites (the star icon on a module or record) let you pin frequently used items to a personal shortcuts list, separate from the admin-defined navigator structure.
Navigation.2: Every record can be viewed as a List (many records, columns) or a Form (one record, fields). Breadcrumbs at the top of a list show the filter/condition currently applied, and clicking a breadcrumb segment lets you refine or remove that condition. History (the clock icon) tracks recently visited records so you can jump back quickly. User preferences -- theme, list row count, and default landing page -- are set from the profile menu, not System Properties.

Platform Admin.1: System Properties (sys_properties table, usually reached via specific admin modules rather than one central list) store instance-wide configuration -- things like date formats, default currency, and feature toggles. Plugins are packaged features you activate per-instance; activating one can add tables, roles, UI, and data. The Application Menu structure (sys_app_application / modules) is what builds the left navigator itself, so admins customize navigation by editing menus and modules directly.
Platform Admin.2: System Logs (System Logs > System Log > All) capture errors, script output, and security events for troubleshooting -- filter by level (Error/Warning/Info) and source. Scheduled Jobs run background processes on a timer (e.g., nightly cleanup); admins can view run history and next-run time. List personalization (the gear icon on a list) lets a user reorder/add/remove columns for their own view without changing the list for everyone else.

Users, Groups & Roles.1: A user record (sys_user table) has an Active checkbox -- inactive users can't log in but their historical records (tickets they created/were assigned) stay intact. Locked out and password reset flags also live here. User preferences (time zone, date format, notification preferences) are stored per-user and override system defaults.
Users, Groups & Roles.2: Groups (sys_user_group) bundle users for assignment purposes -- an assignment group on a ticket, not an individual, is usually the actual owner until someone picks it up. Group type further categorizes groups (e.g., itil, catalog) for filtering in assignment lookups. A user can belong to multiple groups, and group membership drives both notifications and list filters like 'My Groups' Work.
Users, Groups & Roles.3: Roles (sys_user_role) grant capabilities and are checked by ACLs -- a user's effective roles are the union of roles assigned directly plus roles contained by their groups. Roles can themselves contain other roles (role inheritance), so granting one high-level role can implicitly grant several lower-level ones. The admin role bypasses most ACLs entirely, which is why it's tightly restricted in real instances.

Security & ACL.1: Access Control Lists (sys_security_acl) are the platform's core permission model, evaluated at three levels: table (all records), record/field-specific overrides, and individual field. Each ACL targets one operation -- read, write, create, or delete -- so a table typically has several ACL records covering different operations, not one ACL that does everything.
Security & ACL.2: Each ACL has 'Requires role' (any role in the list is sufficient -- OR logic) and an optional script condition (a Condition builder or an Advanced script returning true/false -- AND logic with the role check). Both the role check and the script must pass for access to be granted; if no roles are listed, the role check is treated as satisfied for anyone.
Security & ACL.3: ACLs are evaluated using table inheritance -- an ACL defined on the base 'task' table applies to Incident, Problem, and Change unless a more specific ACL exists on that child table, in which case the more specific one wins. The wildcard table entry ('*') acts as a last-resort fallback ACL when no table- or field-specific ACL matches at all.
Security & ACL.4: Data Policies enforce mandatory/read-only rules server-side (and can mirror them client-side), similar in effect to UI Policies but evaluated regardless of which client is used (browser, mobile, integration) -- ACLs control whether you can see/touch a field or record at all, while Data Policies and UI Policies control the state of a field you're already allowed to access.

Forms.1: A form is built from sections (groups of fields, sometimes tabbed) plus related lists at the bottom showing child records (e.g., Incident's related Problem, or an Approval's related items). Form Design lets an admin drag fields on/off a form per view (Default, Mobile, etc.) without touching the underlying dictionary -- a field can exist on the table but be hidden from a specific form view.
Forms.2: UI Policies run client-side to conditionally show/hide, make mandatory, or make read-only one or more fields based on a condition (e.g., 'Category = Hardware' makes 'Asset Tag' mandatory). Unlike Data Policies, a UI Policy's mandatory/read-only settings apply only in the browser form -- they don't block a save coming from script or integration unless 'Enforce on client and server' style options or a matching Data Policy is also in place.
Forms.3: UI Actions are buttons, links, or context-menu items (e.g., 'Resolve Incident') that run a script when clicked -- they can be scoped to a form, a list, or both, and conditioned on role or a script condition just like other configurable elements. Client Scripts are different: they run automatically on form events (onLoad, onChange, onSubmit) rather than requiring a click, and are the usual place for the JavaScript behind UI-Policy-like custom behavior that's too complex for the no-code UI Policy builder.

Lists & Filters.1: A list view shows selected columns for a set of records; the columns shown are controlled per-list (and per-user, if personalized) independently of the form layout. The gear/list-personalization icon lets a user add, remove, and reorder columns without needing admin rights, while List Layout (an admin-only variant) sets the default for everyone.
Lists & Filters.2: The filter/condition builder combines conditions with AND/OR, grouped with parentheses for more complex logic (e.g., 'State = New OR (State = In Progress AND Priority = 1)'). Related lists on a form can carry their own filter, independent of the parent list's filter, to only surface relevant child records.
Lists & Filters.3: Breadcrumbs above a filtered list show the active condition in plain language -- clicking a breadcrumb segment opens it for editing, and an 'x' removes just that condition. A useful filter can be saved as a Favorite for one-click reuse later, distinct from saving it as a full List View.
Lists & Filters.4: List actions like bulk 'Update Selected' or 'Export' (CSV/Excel/PDF) operate only on the currently filtered/selected rows, not the whole table. A List Collector is a form-field UI element for picking multiple related records (e.g., 'Additional assignees') and is a different concept from a related list, which is auto-populated from an existing reference relationship rather than manually chosen.

Data Model.1: Every table in ServiceNow can extend another table, inheriting its fields and, by default, its ACLs. The 'task' table is the classic example -- Incident, Problem, and Change all extend Task and share its core fields (state, priority, assignment_group) while adding their own specific fields.
Data Model.2: The Dictionary (sys_dictionary) defines every field on every table: its type (String, Reference, Choice, etc.), length, mandatory flag, and default value. Field types constrain what a UI Policy or Business Rule can meaningfully do with that field -- e.g., a Reference field points to a record in another table, not free text.
Data Model.3: Reference fields create relationships between tables (e.g., Incident.caller_id references sys_user). Dot-walking lets you traverse that relationship in a filter, report, or script (e.g., caller_id.department.name) without writing a join yourself -- the platform resolves the chain automatically.
Data Model.4: A Database View combines fields from multiple related tables into one read-only virtual table, useful for reporting across a relationship without dot-walking every field manually. This is different from table extension: a database view doesn't create a new record type, it just presents existing records from joined tables together.

CMDB.1: The CMDB (Configuration Management Database) stores Configuration Items (CIs) in the cmdb_ci table and its many extended classes (cmdb_ci_server, cmdb_ci_app_server, etc.) -- mirroring the task/Incident extension pattern, each CI class inherits base fields and can add class-specific ones (e.g., a server CI has CPU/RAM fields a generic CI doesn't).
CMDB.2: Relationships between CIs (cmdb_rel_ci) capture how items depend on or connect to each other -- e.g., 'Runs on::Runs' between an application and the server hosting it. These relationships power dependency views and impact analysis, letting you see what else might break if a given CI goes down.
CMDB.3: Discovery and Service Mapping are the tools that populate and keep the CMDB accurate by scanning the network and applications (conceptual knowledge for the exam, not hands-on in this app since it requires real infrastructure). CI reconciliation rules decide which data source 'wins' when multiple discovery sources report conflicting values for the same CI attribute.

Business Logic.1: Business Rules run server-side, triggered by database operations, at one of several timings: before (can modify/abort the operation), after (runs once the change is committed), async (runs in the background, non-blocking), or display (runs when the form loads, passing values to the client). Order matters when multiple rules apply to the same table/operation -- lower order numbers run first.
Business Logic.2: Script Includes hold reusable server-side JavaScript classes/functions, callable from Business Rules, other Script Includes, UI Actions, and more -- writing shared logic once here instead of copy-pasting it into every Business Rule is the standard best practice. They're not directly callable from client-side code unless explicitly exposed via GlideAjax.
Business Logic.3: GlideRecord is the core server-side API for querying and modifying records: instantiate it against a table, add query conditions, call query(), then loop with next() to read fields or set them and call update()/insert(). Nearly every custom Business Rule and Script Include touches GlideRecord in some form.
Business Logic.4: Events (fired via gs.eventQueue() or GlideRecord.insert/update in some patterns) decouple 'something happened' from 'do something about it' -- a Business Rule can raise an event, and separate Script Actions or Notifications listen for that specific event name and react independently. This keeps the triggering logic simple while allowing multiple unrelated reactions to the same event.

Update Sets.1: An Update Set captures configuration changes (not data) -- new fields, business rules, UI policies, and similar customizations -- as you make them, so they can be tracked and moved as a bundle. The 'Default' update set exists on every instance; best practice is to create and switch to a dedicated custom update set before starting real configuration work, so changes are easy to isolate and review.
Update Sets.2: To move changes between instances (e.g., dev to test), an Update Set is exported/retrieved on the target instance and then Previewed, which checks for conflicts (e.g., a field that was also changed independently on the target). Conflicts must be resolved before the set can be Committed, which is the step that actually applies the captured changes to the target instance.

Service Catalog.1: Catalog items are the individual requestable things in the Service Catalog (e.g., 'Request a Laptop'), organized into Categories for browsing. Each item has Variables -- the input fields a requester fills out -- which come in types like Single Line Text, Reference, Checkbox, and Multi-Row Variable Set for grouping related variables together.
Service Catalog.2: Catalog UI Policies are the Service-Catalog-specific equivalent of regular UI Policies, but they apply to variables and only take effect within the catalog item's request form (not on the resulting record's own form later). They're configured on the catalog item itself, separate from a UI Policy on the underlying table.
Service Catalog.3: Order Guides bundle multiple catalog items into a single guided request flow (e.g., 'New Hire Package' orders a laptop, a monitor, and software access together). Catalog Client Scripts run on the catalog item's request form (onLoad/onChange/onSubmit, same event model as regular client scripts) to control variable behavior before the request is even submitted.
Service Catalog.4: A Record Producer lets a catalog request create a record on some other table directly (e.g., an Incident) instead of the standard Requested Item/Catalog Task process -- useful when the fulfillment process is really just 'create this ticket'. Regular catalog items, by contrast, typically flow into a Workflow or Flow Designer flow that orchestrates approvals and fulfillment tasks after the request is placed.

Knowledge Management.1: Knowledge Bases (kb_knowledge_base) organize Knowledge Articles into Categories for browsing and permission scoping -- an article's visibility can be restricted by both the knowledge base's own permissions and the article's individual audience. Articles move through states -- typically Draft, Published, and Retired -- and only Published articles are generally visible/searchable to end users.
Knowledge Management.2: Users can rate and comment on articles (feedback), which helps content owners identify articles that need updating. Knowledge search relevance is influenced by keyword matching plus usage signals like view count and rating. Knowledge articles can also be linked directly to Incidents/Cases so agents can attach a relevant article as part of resolving a ticket.

Reporting.1: Reports pull from a single table (or a database view) and render as one of several types -- List, Bar, Pie, Line/Trend -- chosen based on what you're trying to show (a trend over time needs a time-based grouping, a breakdown by category suits a bar/pie). The Report Designer lets you pick the data source, group/aggregate fields, and filter conditions without writing any code.
Reporting.2: Dashboards combine multiple reports and other widgets (like a Single Score or a List widget) onto one page, and can be shared with specific users, groups, or roles -- report/dashboard sharing is a separate permission layer from the ACLs on the underlying data itself. Reports can also be scheduled for recurring email delivery instead of requiring someone to log in and check the dashboard.

Data Import.1: Import Sets bring external data into a staging table (an Import Set Table, prefixed u_imp_ or similar) before it ever touches your real data tables -- Data Sources define where that data comes from (a file upload, JDBC connection, LDAP, etc.) and how it's loaded into the staging table.
Data Import.2: Transform Maps define how fields on the staging table map to fields on the real target table, including Field Maps (source field to target field) and Transform Scripts (custom logic that runs during the transform, e.g., looking up a reference field by name instead of sys_id).
Data Import.3: Coalesce fields on a field map tell the transform 'if a record with this value already exists, update it instead of creating a duplicate' -- essential for imports that run repeatedly against the same source data. Scheduled Imports automate this whole pipeline (fetch, load into staging, transform) to run on a recurring timer instead of manually each time.

Flow Designer.1: A Flow is the top-level automation built in Flow Designer, made of reusable Actions (single steps, like 'Create Record' or 'Send Notification') and optionally Subflows (a flow called by another flow, for reuse). Every flow starts with a Trigger -- e.g., 'Record Created/Updated' on a table, or a Service Catalog item being ordered -- that decides when the flow runs.
Flow Designer.2: Inside a flow, standard logic blocks like If/Else branches and For Each loops control the path taken, and the Actions library (both out-of-box and custom-built) supplies the steps you drag in between them -- much like a Business Rule's script logic, but built visually instead of in JavaScript.
Flow Designer.3: Flow Designer is the modern replacement for the legacy Workflow Editor (still present on some older instances but not for new automation work) -- a flow must be explicitly Activated before its trigger will fire in the live instance, similar to how an Update Set must be Committed before its changes take effect elsewhere.

Notifications.review: This topic's question pool is small, so instead of a graded block quiz you get hands-on practice: build and trigger a real notification on your own Personal Developer Instance (PDI). Notifications combine a Trigger (an event or record condition), an Email Template (the actual message layout), and 'When to send' conditions that decide whether a given update actually fires the notification.
Notifications.lab_instructions:
1. On your PDI, go to System Notification > Email > Notifications and click New.
2. Set Name to something like 'My Test Incident Notification', Table to Incident, and check 'Send when' > Record inserted.
3. Under 'Who will receive', add yourself (or the Caller field) as a recipient.
4. Under the Message tab, write a short Subject and Message using a field reference like ${number} to confirm dynamic content works.
5. Save, then create a brand-new Incident from Incident > Create New and submit it.
6. Check System Logs > Email > Sent (or your inbox if outbound email is configured on the PDI) to confirm the notification fired.
Notifications.lab_checklist:
- Notification record created with a clear trigger condition
- Email Template or inline message includes at least one dynamic field reference
- New Incident created to trigger the notification
- Confirmed the notification actually sent (via Sent email log or inbox)
- Could explain, in your own words, the difference between the trigger condition and the recipient list

Scripting & Automation.review: This topic's question pool is small, so instead of a graded block quiz you get hands-on practice: write a real Client Script and a real Script Include on your PDI. Client Scripts run in the browser on form events (onLoad, onChange, onSubmit); Script Includes hold reusable server-side logic that Client Scripts can call via GlideAjax without a full page reload.
Scripting & Automation.lab_instructions:
1. On a table like Incident, go to System Definition > Client Scripts and create a new onChange script on the Category field.
2. Write a simple script that shows an alert() or sets another field's value when Category changes -- use g_form.setValue() to confirm you can manipulate the form.
3. Test it by opening a new Incident form and changing Category.
4. Separately, create a Script Include (System Definition > Script Includes) with a simple function, e.g., one that returns a string.
5. Reference GlideRecord basics: write a short Background Script (System Definition > Scripts - Background, use with care, read-only query only) that queries 5 Incidents and gs.info()'s their numbers, to see GlideRecord.query()/next() in action.
Scripting & Automation.lab_checklist:
- Client Script created and confirmed working via a live form test
- Used g_form.setValue() or g_form.getValue() at least once
- Script Include created with at least one callable function
- Background script using GlideRecord query/next executed successfully
- Could explain why Script Includes aren't directly callable from client-side code without GlideAjax

Collaboration.review: This topic's question pool is small, so instead of a graded block quiz you get hands-on practice: try ServiceNow's built-in collaboration tools on your PDI. Connect Chat lets users message each other and discuss a specific record inline; Live Feed is a more public, timeline-style activity stream.
Collaboration.lab_instructions:
1. Open any Incident record on your PDI.
2. Look for the Connect/Collaboration icon (usually top-right of the form) and start a conversation linked to that record.
3. Send a message referencing the record, and note how the conversation stays attached to that specific Incident.
4. Explore Live Feed (if enabled on your PDI) and post an update, then @mention another user (even a test user) to see how mentions work.
5. Check the record's Activity log to see whether the collaboration activity is reflected there.
Collaboration.lab_checklist:
- Started a Connect Chat conversation tied to a specific record
- Sent at least one message referencing record context
- Tried an @mention (in Connect or Live Feed)
- Confirmed whether/how the conversation is visible from the record itself

Virtual Agent.review: This topic's question pool is small, so instead of a graded block quiz you get hands-on practice: build a minimal Virtual Agent topic on your PDI. Virtual Agent uses Topics (conversation flows) built in the Virtual Agent Designer, triggered by NLU intents or direct topic selection, to handle simple requests without a live agent.
Virtual Agent.lab_instructions:
1. Go to Virtual Agent Designer (may require activating the Virtual Agent plugin on your PDI if not already active).
2. Create a new Topic with a simple trigger phrase, e.g., 'check my tickets'.
3. Add a basic conversational step -- a message block, then a step that queries and displays the user's open Incidents.
4. Publish/activate the topic.
5. Test it via the Virtual Agent chat widget (often available from the Service Portal or Now Mobile) by typing your trigger phrase.
Virtual Agent.lab_checklist:
- Topic created with a clear trigger phrase or intent
- At least one conversational step beyond a static message (e.g., a data lookup)
- Topic published/activated
- Tested live via the chat widget and confirmed the expected response

ITSM & SLA.review: This topic's question pool is small, so instead of a graded block quiz you get hands-on practice: create an Incident and watch an SLA timer run against it in real time on your PDI. SLA Definitions specify start/pause/stop conditions and a duration; breaches and warning percentages are visible on the record via the SLA related list.
ITSM & SLA.lab_instructions:
1. Create a new Incident with Priority set to a value that has an existing SLA Definition (P1 usually has an aggressive SLA on most PDIs).
2. Save the record, then check the SLA related list at the bottom of the form -- you should see an active SLA with a running timer/percentage.
3. Change the Incident's State to something that should pause the SLA (e.g., 'On Hold', if configured) and observe whether the timer pauses.
4. Look up the actual SLA Definition record (SLA > SLA Definitions) referenced by your Incident's SLA, and read through its Start/Pause/Stop conditions to see what actually drives that behavior.
ITSM & SLA.lab_checklist:
- Incident created that attached an active SLA
- Observed the SLA's running percentage/timer on the record
- Tested a state change that should pause or stop the SLA
- Reviewed the underlying SLA Definition's conditions, not just the running instance

Testing/ATF.review: This topic's question pool is small, so instead of a graded block quiz you get hands-on practice: build a simple Automated Test Framework (ATF) test on your PDI. ATF tests are made of Test Steps (form actions, assertions, server-side steps) chained together, and can be grouped into Test Suites for batch running.
Testing/ATF.lab_instructions:
1. Go to Automated Test Framework > Tests and create a new test, e.g., 'Create Incident Test'.
2. Add a Test Step: 'Open a New Form' targeting Incident.
3. Add another step: 'Set Field Values' (e.g., set Short Description to a test string).
4. Add a 'Submit Form' step, then an assertion step like 'Field Value Assertion' to confirm the Incident number field is now populated.
5. Save and run the test, then check the Test Results to see pass/fail per step.
Testing/ATF.lab_checklist:
- Test created with at least one form-interaction step
- At least one assertion step included (not just actions)
- Test executed and results reviewed
- Could explain the difference between a single Test and a Test Suite

Agile/VTB.review: This topic's question pool is small, so instead of a graded block quiz you get hands-on practice: set up a basic Visual Task Board (VTB) on your PDI. Agile Development in ServiceNow uses Stories, Epics, and Sprints for planning, while VTB gives a Kanban-style visual board for moving cards (Stories, Tasks) between status columns.
Agile/VTB.lab_instructions:
1. Go to Visual Task Boards and create a new board based on a table like Story or Incident.
2. Add columns representing your workflow states (e.g., To Do, In Progress, Done), mapped to actual field values.
3. Create a couple of test records (e.g., Stories) so they appear as cards on the board.
4. Drag a card between columns and confirm the underlying record's field actually updates to match.
5. If Agile plugin is active, briefly look at Agile Development > Stories/Sprints to see how Stories relate to a Sprint, separate from the VTB view of them.
Agile/VTB.lab_checklist:
- VTB board created and mapped to a real table/field
- At least one column configured to reflect a real status value
- Card dragged between columns with the underlying record confirmed updated
- Could explain the difference between a VTB board and a Sprint/Story planning view
